planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring into well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; full sun is essential for best flowering and disease resistance.

directions: Carolina Rose is a slender, drought-tolerant native of dry open woods, meadows, and roadsides throughout eastern North America, thriving in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soils where more vigorous shrubs struggle. Plant rooted cuttings or suckers in spring into a sunny, open site, spacing plants 3 to 5 feet apart to allow air circulation and natural colony spread. In June, the arching stems bear fragrant, single, pale to deep pink flowers about 1.5 to 2 inches across, followed by small, persistent red rose hips that feed mockingbirds, thrushes, and waxwings well into winter. No spraying or heavy pruning is needed; simply remove dead wood in late winter and allow the plant to spread slowly by suckers into an informal, wildlife-friendly thicket.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rosa carolina

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil; ideal for rain garden edges, stream banks, and pond margins.

directions: Swamp Rose is a robust, upright-arching native rose naturally found along stream banks, swamp margins, and moist open meadows from Nova Scotia to Florida, and is the most flood-tolerant of all native eastern rose species. Plant rooted cuttings in spring into moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade, spacing plants 4 to 6 feet apart to accommodate their vigorous suckering habit. Deep rose-pink, 5-petaled, fragrant flowers 1.5 to 2 inches across appear on recurved-prickled arching canes from June through July, followed by round red hips that persist through winter and are eaten by dozens of bird species. Allow plants to spread naturally by suckers to colonize wet areas, stream banks, and rain garden berms where few other flowering shrubs will thrive.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rosa palustris

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into dry, sandy, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun; excellent for coastal plain sites and old fields where other blackberries fail.

directions: Sand Blackberry is a low-growing native blackberry of dry, sandy, acidic open ground across the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and Texas, identified by its broadly wedge-shaped leaflets with distinctive white-felted undersides. Plant rooted cuttings in spring into dry, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun, spacing plants 3 to 5 feet apart to allow for natural spreading by root sprouts into a dense, wildlife-supporting thicket. White flowers appear in spring on second-year canes, followed by small but sweet, glossy black drupes in early summer that are excellent for jams, pies, and fresh eating. Remove spent floricanes after harvest each season and allow vigorous new primocanes to develop; this species is far more drought-tolerant than Rubus allegheniensis or R. argutus and thrives where heavier soils would cause root rot.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Rubus cuneifolius

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; space generously to allow for vigorous root-sprout spread and airflow between canes.

directions: Allegheny Blackberry is one of the most productive and widely distributed wild blackberries of eastern North America, growing in open fields, roadsides, forest edges, and disturbed ground from Nova Scotia south to Georgia. Plant rooted cuttings in spring into well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun, spacing plants 4 to 6 feet apart; plants spread rapidly by root sprouts and will form dense, wildlife-supporting thickets if not managed. White flowers appear in spring on stout, heavily prickled, arching second-year canes, followed by large, firm, sweet black drupes from June through July that rank among the finest-flavored of any native blackberry. After harvest, cut spent floricanes to the ground to encourage strong new primocanes; very cold-hardy plants will begin reliable production in their second full year.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rubus allegheniensis

planting.note: Plant rooted tip cuttings in spring into dry, sandy or rocky, well-drained soil in full sun; ideal as a low ground-covering berry plant for thin-soiled banks and barrens.

directions: Northern Dewberry is a low-trailing native of dry, open, sandy or rocky ground and thin-soiled barrens throughout eastern North America, distinguished from upright blackberries by its prostrate, creeping canes that root at the tips to form low, dense, ground-covering mats. Plant rooted tip cuttings in spring into dry, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun, spacing plants 2 to 3 feet apart; individual canes will trail and root freely to expand coverage over a wide area. White flowers appear in spring and ripen into sweet, black, aggregate drupes in late May to June, making Northern Dewberry one of the earliest-fruiting native brambles and a critical early-summer food source for bluebirds, mockingbirds, towhees, and box turtles. This species excels in sandy, infertile, full-sun sites where taller brambles and most garden fruits cannot survive, making it a uniquely useful edible ground cover for difficult dry spots.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rubus flagellaris
